text:
Glia brain tumors are a common type of cancer whose early detection results in higher survival rates. While it is possible to make a definitive diagnosis of these tumours after removal of a sample of the tumour by surgery, this is a very invasive process, especially in a critical organ such as the brain. It is therefore of great interest to be able to carry out an accurate non-invasive diagnosis of gliomas, meningiomas and pituitary gland tumours, which motivated the development and assessment of novel computational intelligence approaches for the automated classification of tumours based on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). The work reported here is based on a training dataset of 3264 samples of T1-weighted contrast-enhanced MRI scans of healthy brains as well as brains with gliomas, meningiomas and pituitary gland tumours.

This study employed an intense data-preprocessing and augmentation procedure for the acquisition of high-quality data sets, which were then classified using two sophisticated deep learning networks that were designed in this study: a custom hierarchical 2D-Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and a modified convolutional auto-encoder. The 2D-CNN had an 8-layers of convolutional layers with $2 \times 2$ kernels, batch normalization and dropout in order to obtain the best possible feature extraction. Auto-encoders are a type of unsupervised learning network and are composed of two stages: a reconstruction stage and a classification stage, where the diagnostic information lies in the encoder part. We compared the performance of our proposed methods with six common machine learning algorithms in the literature, such as K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Random Forest and Support Vector Machines (SVM) in order to validate our results. Deep learning methods are proved to be more accurate than the conventional machine learning methods and the developed networks can serve as a decision support tool for radiologists for the accurate and fast tumor detection.
explanation: The text is coherent and reads like an academic abstract with plausible technical details, but it contains generic claims and slightly awkward phrasing that are often seen in AI-generated content.
